Jesus enters Judea and immediately confronts the deep entanglement of human desires with God's original design. When the Pharisees press Him on divorce, asking whether a man may cast off his wife for any reason, Jesus refuses to negotiate with their assumptions. He reaches past Moses to the beginning itself, back to creation, where God made male and female and designed them for permanent union. Yes, Moses allowed divorce certificates—but only as a concession to human hardness of heart, not as God's will. What God has joined, no one should tear apart. The disciples grasp the weight of this teaching immediately and voice their real fear: if marriage is this binding, perhaps it is better not to marry at all. Jesus does not soften His answer. Instead, He acknowledges that not everyone can receive this word—that some are called to singleness for the kingdom of heaven's sake. He is not saying marriage is optional or that everyone should abandon it. He is saying that the kingdom of God sometimes demands a willingness to forgo even the deepest human bonds. This is the cost He places before His followers without apology.

Then Jesus turns His attention to the little children brought to Him for blessing, and the disciples move to send them away. Jesus stops them with unmistakable urgency: "Let the little children come to Me." The kingdom belongs to such as these. The contrast is striking. He has just told His disciples that following Him might mean leaving behind wife, children, parents, and possessions. Yet here He welcomes children as models of kingdom belonging. There is no contradiction—children represent those who come to Him without self-reliance, without demanding their own terms, without the confidence that they can earn their way into His favor. They come as receivers, not negotiators.

This becomes the lens through which the rich young man's encounter must be read. He arrives asking what good thing he must do to obtain eternal life—the question already reveals a fundamental misunderstanding. Jesus directs him to the commandments, and the man claims he has kept them all. Then Jesus speaks the word that strips away his self-assurance: "If you want to be perfect, go, sell your possessions and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow Me." The young man walks away grieving because he owned great wealth. He could not receive the kingdom as a little child receives it—by surrendering everything he trusted in. His wealth had become his god, and no amount of commandment-keeping could dislodge it. When Jesus tells His disciples that it is harder for a rich man to enter the kingdom than for a camel to pass through a needle's eye, He is not commenting on wealth as such. He is naming the idol that money becomes—the belief that we can secure our own future, achieve our own standing, keep ourselves safe. This is the hardness of heart that divorce reveals, that riches conceal, that prevents entry into God's kingdom.

But Jesus ends not with impossibility but with restoration. "With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible." This is the heart of the gospel. Salvation is not achieved; it is received. And those who have truly left everything—houses, family, fields, security—for His sake will receive far more than they surrendered. Peter and the other disciples, who had already made that choice when they left their nets to follow Him, will sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes. The last will be first, and the first will be last. The only way into the kingdom is the way of a little child: empty-handed, trusting, surrendered. Everything else—commandments, sacrifices, renunciations—flows from that posture of radical dependence on God's grace alone.
